response: No, it is not possible to rotate these light sources to illuminate the entire plane. Each of the six point light sources emits a beam covering exactly a $60^{\circ}$ sector. The total angular coverage provided by all six sources combined is $6 \times 60^{\circ} = 360^{\circ}$. While this sum equals the full circle, this calculation assumes that the sectors can be arranged perfectly without any overlap and that they cover every direction from a single central point. However, the light sources are located at six distinct, separate points on the plane. To illuminate the entire plane, every point in the infinite 2D space must be covered. Consider a point $P$ located far away from the cluster of the six sources. The angular width of the cluster of sources as seen from $P$ becomes very small. For $P$ to be illuminated, at least one beam must be directed precisely at it. More critically, consider the region between the sources. Because the sources are distinct, there are gaps in coverage unless the beams overlap significantly. If the beams do not overlap, the total coverage is exactly $360^{\circ}$ of angular measure from the perspective of a hypothetical observer at the "center" of the arrangement, but this does not guarantee coverage of the entire 2D area. Specifically, if we arrange the beams to cover a full $360^{\circ}$ circle around the centroid of the points, the beams will inevitably overlap or leave gaps because the sources are not co-located. If the beams are arranged to cover $360^{\circ}$ without overlap, they must originate from the same point to cover a continuous ring; since they originate from different points, there will be regions of the plane (specifically the areas "behind" the sources relative to the beam direction or in the gaps between diverging beams) that remain unlit. Even if we try to cover the plane by overlapping beams, the total angular measure available is fixed. A more rigorous argument involves the concept of covering the plane with sectors. To cover the entire plane, every ray emanating from any point in the plane must be intersected by a beam. If we look at the directions, the sum of the angles is $360^{\circ}$.
